turns.speaker: user
turns.text: please write an academic reflection using Gibbs' model of reflection including analysis and synthesis. please reflect on how the history taking and physical examination university module as effected how I see and treat patients in the minor injuries area of an Emergency department

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text:
Title: Reflection on History-Taking and Physical Examination Module using Gibbs' Model of Reflection

Introduction

This academic reflection aims to evaluate my learning experiences and outcomes during the history-taking and physical examination university module, focusing on its impact on my practice in the minor injuries area of an Emergency department. Gibbs' model of reflection will be used as a guide to structure this reflection, as it offers a systematic approach to exploring experiences and allows for in-depth analysis and synthesis (Gibbs, 1988).

Description

The history-taking and physical examination module provided the opportunity to explore the fundamental aspects of patient assessment, including communication, information gathering, and clinical evaluation. This module was designed to enhance my clinical skills, enabling me to carry out comprehensive patient assessments and develop appropriate care plans in the minor injuries area of the Emergency department.

Thoughts and Feelings

During the module, I felt both excited and nervous about learning new skills and applying them to real-life clinical situations. The educational setting allowed me to develop a deeper understanding of the importance of thorough patient assessment, which focuses on active listening and effective communication to obtain an accurate history.

Moreover, the hands-on approach with the physical examination component provided a safe environment for me to build my confidence in applying advanced clinical skills on real patients. Overall, I felt more equipped to handle various patients with minor injuries effectively.

Evaluation

The module has positively impacted my clinical practice in several ways. Firstly, I am more attuned to the importance of taking a comprehensive and systematic approach during patient assessment. My ability to gather pertinent information and ask focused questions enables me to develop a more targeted management plan for minor injuries, ensuring appropriate care and improved patient outcomes.

Secondly, I am now more confident in my physical examination skills, which allows me to identify and address critical injuries more effectively. Thirdly, I have noticed an improvement in my communication and interpersonal skills, which has further strengthened my rapport with patients and fostered trust, ensuring a positive patient experience.

Analysis

Upon reflecting on my improved patient assessment skills, I realize that good communication with patients is essential for accurate history-taking (Kurtz et al., 2005). This has been evidenced in my practice where effective communication has allowed for effective management of patients in the minor injuries area.

Synthesis

Through this module, I developed critical skills in history-taking and physical examination, applicable to my clinical practice in the minor injuries area of the Emergency department. I acknowledge there are continuous opportunities for improvement, which will require ongoing professional development and self-reflective practice.

Integrating the skills I have acquired, I now aim to focus my ongoing learning and development on improving other facets of my clinical practice, such as empathy, clinical reasoning, and decision-making skills. By continuously reflecting on my experiences, using models like Gibbs', I can foster personal and professional growth, ultimately leading to improved patient care and outcomes.

Conclusion

The history-taking and physical examination university module has played a significant role in influencing how I see and treat patients in the minor injuries area of an Emergency department. Through successes and challenges, I have enhanced my assessment skills and broadened my understanding of the patient experience. Applying Gibbs' model of reflection has enabled me to focus on key areas for improvement and offers a structured approach to analyzing and synthesizing my learning experiences in the context of my clinical practice.

References

Gibbs, G. (1988). Learning by doing: A guide to teaching and learning methods. Oxford: Oxford Polytechnic.

Kurtz, S., Silverman, J., & Draper, J. (2005). Teaching and learning communication skills in medicine. Abingdon: Radcliffe Publishing.

turns.text: please write a critical refection using Gibbs' model of reflection about how answering multiple choice questions before and after learning about history taking and physical examination has allowed me to assess how my knowledge has improved and cite relevant academic references throughout.

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text:
Title: Critical Reflection on Answering Multiple Choice Questions and the Improvement in History-taking and Physical Examination Knowledge using Gibbs' Model of Reflection

Introduction

This critical reflection will use Gibbs' Model of Reflection (1988) to analyze the impact of answering multiple-choice questions (MCQs) before and after learning about history-taking and physical examination on assessing my knowledge improvement. This model offers a structured framework to explore experiences and facilitate deep analysis and synthesis of learning outcomes.

Description

During the module, I participated in answering MCQs on history-taking and physical examination before and after the teaching sessions. The pre-module MCQs aimed to assess my initial knowledge of the topics, while the post-module MCQs were designed to evaluate the knowledge gained from the module. This exercise allowed me to identify areas of strength and areas requiring further improvement.

Thoughts and Feelings

Initially, I felt apprehensive about the MCQ tests, doubting my abilities and fearing a poor performance. However, the pre-module MCQ results demonstrated that my knowledge on history taking and physical examination was generally adequate but still required enhancement, which made me feel motivated to engage in learning sessions and improve my skills. After completing the learning sessions, I felt more confident and eager to attempt the post-module MCQs, in anticipation of evidence of my improved knowledge.

Evaluation

Overall, answering MCQs before and after the learning sessions proved to be a valuable exercise in guiding and assessing my improvement in history-taking and physical examination. Research in medical education highlights the importance of formative assessment for learning enhancement (Veloski et al., 2006), and the results of my pre-and post-module MCQs served as a direct reflection of my improved understanding of effective patient assessment and examination techniques.

Analysis

Upon reviewing my MCQ results and identifying areas of improvement, I realized the impact of targeted learning approaches on closing the gaps in my knowledge (Nicol & Macfarlane-Dick, 2006). The pre-module MCQs allowed me to identify the areas in which I lacked understanding, thus enabling me to focus on the relevant topics during the learning sessions.

Moreover, the post-module MCQs demonstrated that my knowledge and understanding of history-taking and physical examination improved significantly during the module. This corresponds with the literature emphasizing the necessity of feedback for effective learning (Hattie & Timperley, 2007).

Synthesis

Through the use of MCQs as a formative assessment tool, I achieved a better understanding of history-taking and physical examination and identified the gaps in my knowledge. In the future, I intend to employ similar assessment strategies to ensure continuous improvement and enhance my clinical practice. Additionally, I will engage in reflective practice, using Gibbs' Model, to monitor my progress and inform my learning goals and plans.

Conclusion

In conclusion, answering MCQs before and after the module on history-taking and physical examination allowed me to assess my knowledge improvement effectively. Employing formative assessment strategies, such as MCQs, and integrating reflective practice models, such as Gibbs', plays an essential role in fostering professional development and enhancing clinical skills. By engaging in continuous assessment and reflection, I can ensure the consistent evolution of my knowledge and practice moving forward.

References

Gibbs, G. (1988). Learning by doing: A guide to teaching and learning methods. Oxford: Oxford Polytechnic.

Hattie, J., & Timperley, H. (2007). The power of feedback. Review of Educational Research, 77(1), 81-112.

Nicol, D. J., & Macfarlane-Dick, D. (2006). Formative assessment and self-regulated learning: A model and seven principles of good feedback practice. Studies in Higher Education, 31(2), 199-218.

Veloski, J., Boex, J. R., Grasberger, M. J., Evans, A., & Wolfson, D. B. (2006). Systematic review of the literature on assessment, feedback and physicians' clinical performance: BEME Guide No. 7. Medical Teacher, 28(2), 117-128.

turns.text: Please write a critical academic reflection on how completing pre course multiple choice questions regarding body systems could have improved my learning during the history taking and physical examination University module.

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text:
Title: Critical Reflection on the Impact of Pre-Course Multiple Choice Questions on Learning Outcomes during History Taking and Physical Examination Module

Introduction

This critical academic reflection will explore the potential impact of completing pre-course multiple-choice questions (MCQs) on body systems in enhancing learning outcomes during the history-taking and physical examination University module. Through the lens of reflective practice, I will analyze the effectiveness of this formative assessment tool and discuss the implications for my learning process.

Description

Prior to commencing the history-taking and physical examination university module, I completed a pre-course assessment consisting of MCQs related to the body systems. The purpose of this exercise was to evaluate my foundational knowledge and assess any gaps that could be addressed during the module. The pre-course MCQs provided a baseline understanding of my existing knowledge on various body systems and their relevance to history-taking and physical examinations.

Thoughts and Feelings

Initially, I was apprehensive about completing the pre-course MCQs, as I felt unsure of my current knowledge and abilities in relation to body systems. Despite these feelings, I recognized the potential benefits of this formative assessment in directing my learning during the module. As I completed the MCQs, I became more aware of my knowledge gaps, which motivated me to actively engage in learning and address these shortcomings during the module.

Evaluation

Overall, the pre-course MCQs served as an effective method for identifying areas requiring improvement in my knowledge of body systems. Research has demonstrated the positive impact of formative assessment on student learning (Nicol & Macfarlane-Dick, 2006). By completing these MCQs, I was better equipped to focus on specific aspects of body systems during the history taking and physical examination module, resulting in a more targeted and efficient learning experience.

Analysis

The pre-course MCQs facilitated my ability to recognize my knowledge gaps and target my learning during the module. The literature supports the notion that identifying areas for improvement enables students to direct their learning more effectively (Black & Wiliam, 1998). By assessing my initial understanding of body systems, the pre-course MCQs allowed me to identify the areas I needed to focus on during the module, thereby setting clear learning objectives and guiding my learning process.

Synthesis

Reflecting on the impact of pre-course MCQs, I realize that incorporating formative assessment strategies is essential for fostering self-regulated learning and maximizing the effectiveness of educational experiences. Moving forward, I plan to continue using formative assessments, such as MCQs, to guide my learning throughout my academic and professional career. Additionally, reflective practice models, such as Gibbs', will be utilized to facilitate ongoing evaluation and development of my knowledge and skills.

Conclusion

Completing pre-course MCQs on body systems significantly influenced my learning experience during the history-taking and physical examination university module. By identifying knowledge gaps, these assessments allowed me to tailor my learning objectives and engage more effectively in the module. Incorporating formative assessment strategies and reflective practice into my educational journey is crucial for achieving continuous improvement and enhancing my clinical abilities.

References

Black, P., & Wiliam, D. (1998). Assessment and classroom learning. Assessment in Education: Principles, Policy & Practice, 5(1), 7-74.

Nicol, D. J., & Macfarlane-Dick, D. (2006). Formative assessment and self-regulated learning: A model and seven principles of good feedback practice. Studies in Higher Education, 31(2), 199-218.

Title: Critical Reflection on the Impact of Pre-Course Multiple Choice Questions on Learning Outcomes during the History Taking and Physical Examination University Module

Introduction

This critical academic reflection will explore the potential impact of completing pre-course multiple-choice questions (MCQs) on body systems in enhancing learning outcomes during the history-taking and physical examination University module. Building on the reflective practice, I will analyze the effectiveness of this formative assessment tool and discuss the implications for my learning process.

Description

Prior to commencing the history-taking and physical examination University module, I completed a pre-course assessment consisting of MCQs related to body systems. The purpose of this exercise was to evaluate my foundational knowledge and assess any gaps that could be addressed during the module. The pre-course MCQs provided a baseline understanding of my existing knowledge on various body systems and their relevance to history-taking and physical examinations.

Thoughts and Feelings

Initially, I was apprehensive about completing the pre-course MCQs, as I felt unsure of my current knowledge and abilities in relation to body systems. Despite these feelings, I recognized the potential benefits of this formative assessment in directing my learning during the module. As I completed the MCQs, I became more aware of my knowledge gaps, which motivated me to actively engage in learning and address these shortcomings during the module.

Evaluation

Overall, the pre-course MCQs served as an effective method for identifying areas requiring improvement in my knowledge of body systems. A meta-analysis conducted by Evans (2013) emphasized the positive impact of formative assessment on student learning. By completing these MCQs, I was better equipped to focus on specific aspects of body systems during the history taking and physical examination module, resulting in a more targeted and efficient learning experience.

Analysis

The pre-course MCQs facilitated my ability to recognize my knowledge gaps and target my learning during the module. Cantillon and Sargeant (2008) highlighted the importance of formative assessment in identifying areas for improvement and guiding student learning more effectively. By assessing my initial understanding of body systems, the pre-course MCQs allowed me to identify the areas I needed to focus on during the module, thereby setting clear learning objectives and guiding my learning process.

Synthesis

Reflecting on the impact of pre-course MCQs, I realize that incorporating formative assessment strategies is essential for fostering self-regulated learning (Panadero, 2017) and maximizing the effectiveness of educational experiences. Moving forward, I plan to continue using formative assessments, such as MCQs, to guide my learning throughout my academic and professional career. Additionally, reflective practice models, including reflective writing (Sandars, 2009), will be utilized to facilitate ongoing evaluation and development of my knowledge and skills.

Conclusion

Completing pre-course MCQs on body systems significantly influenced my learning experience during the history-taking and physical examination University module. By identifying knowledge gaps, these assessments allowed me to tailor my learning objectives and engage more effectively in the module. Incorporating formative assessment strategies and reflective practice into my educational journey is crucial for achieving continuous improvement (Van der Vleuten et al., 2010) and enhancing my clinical abilities.

References

Cantillon, P., & Sargeant, J. (2008). Teaching rounds: giving feedback in clinical settings. BMJ, 337, a1961.

Evans, C. (2013). Making sense of assessment feedback in higher education. Review of Educational Research, 83(1), 70-120.

Panadero, E. (2017). A review of self-regulated learning: Six models and four directions for research. Frontiers in Psychology, 8, 422.

Sandars, J. (2009). The use of reflection in medical education: AMEE Guide No. 44. Medical Teacher, 31(8), 685-695.

Van der Vleuten, C. P., Schuwirth, L. W., Driessen, E. W., Dijkstra, J., Tigelaar, D., Baartman, L. K., & Van Tartwijk, J. (2010). A model for programmatic assessment fit for purpose. Medical Teacher, 34(3), 205-214.
